title: Graduated Licensing Stages
summary: Nova Scotia's GDL system moves new drivers through learner, newly licensed, and restricted individual stages. Each stage limits risk while drivers build experience.

title: Restricted Individual Stage
summary: The restricted individual stage lasts two full years after the newly licensed stage. Drivers must have zero alcohol or drugs in the body and cannot supervise another driver.

title: Yielding to Transit Buses
summary: When a stopped transit bus signals to re-enter traffic, drivers approaching from behind must yield where the posted limit is 60 km/h or less and it is safe to do so.

title: Using the Handbook
summary: The handbook explains the basic driving laws in plain language, but drivers are still responsible for knowing current rules and staying alert for updates.

title: Class 7 Eligibility
summary: A Nova Scotia resident can apply for a Class 7 learner's licence at age 16. Applicants who are 16 or 17 need written consent from a parent or guardian.

title: Knowledge Test Pass Mark
summary: The learner test includes a road sign test and a rules test. Each part has 20 multiple-choice questions, and 16 correct answers are required to pass each part.

title: Learner Restrictions
summary: Learners must drive with an experienced driver in the front seat and no other passengers. Their blood alcohol level must remain zero.

title: Demerit Points
summary: Demerit points are assigned for Motor Vehicle Act convictions and affect learner and newly licensed drivers sooner than experienced drivers. Serious offences can delay progress through GDL.

title: Traffic Signals
summary: Green means proceed only when safe, yellow means stop if you can do so safely, and red means stop before entering the intersection. A flashing red works like a stop sign.

title: Crosswalk Priority
summary: Pedestrians have the right of way in marked and unmarked crosswalks at intersections. If a signal changes while someone is crossing, let them finish safely.

title: Intersection Right of Way
summary: A vehicle already in an intersection has priority over one that has not entered. When vehicles arrive together, the driver on the left yields to the driver on the right.

title: Emergency Vehicles
summary: When an emergency vehicle approaches with lights and siren, move to the right edge or curb and stop until it passes. Never follow a responding fire vehicle within 150 metres.

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Regulatory Signs
summary: Regulatory signs state laws and instructions drivers must obey. Stop, yield, turn restrictions, no-entry signs, and many lane-use signs fall into this group.

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Warning Signs
summary: Warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changes ahead, such as curves, merging traffic, hidden intersections, narrow structures, or slippery surfaces.

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Pavement Markings
summary: Yellow lines separate opposing traffic and white lines separate traffic moving the same direction. Solid and broken lines tell drivers when passing or lane changes are allowed.

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Work Zones
summary: Orange work-zone signs warn about temporary hazards, lane changes, workers, and construction traffic. Slow down early, follow instructions, and leave extra space.

title: Vehicle Readiness
summary: Before driving, keep safety equipment such as brakes, lights, tires, mirrors, horn, and wipers in good condition. Regular checks reduce preventable breakdowns and collisions.

title: Seat Belts
summary: Seat belts and child restraints reduce injury risk and help keep occupants inside the vehicle during a sudden stop or collision. Airbags do not replace seat belts.

title: Mirrors and Blind Spots
summary: Adjust mirrors before moving and use them frequently, but remember mirrors do not remove blind spots. Shoulder checks are still needed before changing lanes.

title: Smooth Braking
summary: Brake smoothly and avoid locking the wheels. If your vehicle has ABS, press the brake steadily and firmly rather than pumping it.

title: Night Driving
summary: At night, use headlights properly and reduce speed because you can only see as far as your lights reach. Dim high beams for oncoming or closely followed traffic.

title: Rain, Snow, and Fog
summary: Poor weather reduces visibility and traction. Slow down gradually, avoid sudden stops, increase following distance, and use low-beam headlights when visibility is reduced.

title: Hydroplaning and Ice
summary: Hydroplaning happens when tires ride on water and lose contact with the road. Bridges, overpasses, and shaded areas can freeze before the rest of the road.

title: Brake Failure
summary: If brakes fail, pump the brake pedal several times to build pressure. If that does not work, apply the parking brake gently while holding the release button.

title: Alcohol Impairment
summary: Alcohol-induced impairment begins with the first drink. Legal impairment is defined at 0.08 BAC or more, but drivers can still be charged below that level if impaired.

title: Medication and Drugs
summary: Prescription drugs, non-prescription medication, herbal remedies, and illegal drugs can impair driving. Read warnings and ask a pharmacist when unsure.

title: Warn Range
summary: A roadside screening result between 0.05 and 0.08 may lead to a licence suspension. Learners and GDL drivers must maintain zero alcohol.

title: Plan a Sober Ride
summary: Judgment gets worse as impairment increases, so plan transportation before drinking or using impairing substances. Time is the only reliable way alcohol leaves the body.

title: Road-Ready Vehicle
summary: Before a vehicle is operated on a Nova Scotia highway, it must be registered, permitted, inspected, plated, and insured. Proof documents must be kept available.

title: Registration Basics
summary: A Certificate of Registration shows ownership details such as the owner's name, VIN, issue date, and vehicle description. The certificate remains valid while you own the vehicle.

title: Annual Safety Inspection
summary: Registered vehicles and trailers need an annual safety inspection at an official station. If a vehicle fails, defects must be corrected and reinspected within the allowed period.

title: Insurance Responsibility
summary: Drivers must have liability insurance or another accepted proof of financial responsibility. The handbook lists the legal minimum at $500,000 in third-party liability coverage.

title: Motorcycle Licensing
summary: Motorcycle riders need either a motorcycle driver's licence or an endorsement on a regular licence. New motorcycle riders move through GDL and must pass motorcycle-specific tests.

title: Learner Motorcycle Limits
summary: Learner motorcycle drivers must keep zero blood alcohol, carry no passengers, and ride only from one half-hour before sunrise to one half-hour after sunset.

title: Motorcycle Visibility
summary: Motorcyclists should avoid other drivers' blind spots and choose a lane position that makes them visible. Each motorcycle is entitled to the full use of a traffic lane.

title: Protective Gear
summary: Motorcycle riders and passengers must wear approved, properly fastened helmets. Eye protection and protective clothing help prevent loss of control and reduce injury.

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Construction / Work Zone Signs
summary: Orange work-zone signs warn about temporary hazards, lane shifts, workers, equipment, and reduced speeds. Slow early, watch for instructions, and leave extra space.

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: School and Playground Signs
summary: School and playground signs warn drivers to watch for children. When children are present, school-zone limits are 30 km/h in 50 km/h areas and 50 km/h where the approach limit is higher.

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Flashing Light Units
summary: Flashing arrow boards and straight bar lamps warn of lane closures, obstructions, or active hazards. Follow the arrow direction or treat a straight bar as caution.

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Railway Signs
summary: Railway advance warning signs and crossbucks mark crossings where drivers must slow, look, listen, and be ready to stop. Stop when lights flash or a gate is down.

chapter: Chapter 3 — Signs, Pavement Markings and Work Zones
title: Information and Direction Signs
summary: Green, blue, and white information signs give route numbers, destination directions, distances, and driver services so you can choose lanes and exits early.
